description: Verified bibliography for the hallucination-induced unauthorized action paper. Every entry has been verified in this session via WebSearch (existence/topic) or WebFetch (numerical/methodological claims).

sources.title: AgentHarm: A Benchmark for Measuring Harmfulness of LLM Agents
sources.authors: Andriushchenko, Souly, Dziemian, Duenas, Lin, Wang, Hendrycks, Zou, Kolter, Fredrikson, Winsor, Wynne, Gal, Davies
claims_supported: 110 explicitly malicious agent tasks (440 with augmentations)
11 harm categories including fraud, cybercrime, harassment
Leading LLMs are surprisingly compliant with malicious agent requests without jailbreaking
Universal jailbreak templates effectively jailbreak agents while retaining capabilities

sources.title: Identifying the Risks of LM Agents with an LM-Emulated Sandbox (ToolEmu)
claims_supported: LM-emulated sandbox covering 36 high-stakes toolkits / 144 test cases
Even the safest LM agent fails 23.9% of the time per automatic evaluator
Human evaluation shows 68.8% of identified failures would be valid real-world failures

sources.title: R-Judge: Benchmarking Safety Risk Awareness for LLM Agents
sources.authors: Yuan, He, Dong, Wang, Zhao, Xia, Xu, Zhou, Li, Zhang, Wang, Liu
claims_supported: 569 multi-turn agent interaction records
27 key risk scenarios spanning 5 application categories and 10 risk types
Best-performing model (GPT-4o) achieves 74.42%; no other models significantly exceed random
R-Judge is a meta-evaluation: LLM judging safety from agent interaction records, not agent-side prevention

sources.title: InjecAgent: Benchmarking Indirect Prompt Injections in Tool-Integrated Large Language Model Agents
claims_supported: 1,054 test cases covering 17 user tools and 62 attacker tools
ReAct-prompted GPT-4 vulnerable to indirect prompt injection 24% of the time
Hacking-prompt reinforcement nearly doubles attack success rate

sources.title: SafeAgentBench: A Benchmark for Safe Task Planning of Embodied LLM Agents
claims_supported: 750 task dataset covering 10 potential hazards and 3 task types
Best baseline gets 69% success on safe tasks but only 5% rejection on hazardous tasks
First comprehensive benchmark for safety-aware embodied LLM task planning

sources.title: Jailbreaking LLM-Controlled Robots (RoboPAIR)
claims_supported: Algorithmic jailbreak of LLM-controlled robots demonstrated on NVIDIA Dolphins, Clearpath Jackal (GPT-4o), Unitree Go2 (GPT-3.5)
Often achieves 100% attack success rates eliciting physical harmful actions
Risks of jailbroken LLMs extend beyond text generation to physical damage

sources.title: LLM-based Agents Suffer from Hallucinations: A Survey of Taxonomy, Methods, and Directions
claims_supported: First comprehensive survey of hallucinations in LLM-based agents
Proposes a stage-based taxonomy spanning the agent workflow (brain/perception/action)
Examines 18 triggering causes of agent hallucinations

sources.title: HalluLens: LLM Hallucination Benchmark
sources.authors: Bang, Ji, Schelten, Hartshorn, Fowler, Zhang, Cancedda, Fung
claims_supported: Distinguishes extrinsic from intrinsic hallucinations
Dynamically generates evaluation data so the test set is not leaked
Disentangles hallucination from factuality

sources.title: AGENTIF: Benchmarking Instruction Following of Large Language Models in Agentic Scenarios
claims_supported: Constructed from 50 real-world agentic applications
Instructions average 1,723 words with maximum 15,630; average 11.9 constraints per instruction
Three constraint types: formatting, semantic, tool
707 manually annotated instructions
Best model follows fewer than 30% of instructions perfectly

sources.title: Evaluating LLM Agent Adherence to Hierarchical Safety Principles: A Lightweight Benchmark for Probing Foundational Controllability Components
sources.venue: ICML 2025 Technical AI Governance Workshop submission
claims_supported: Lightweight benchmark for LLM agent adherence to a high-level safety principle under conflicting task instructions
Evaluation of six LLMs reveals a quantifiable 'cost of compliance' and 'illusion of compliance'
Current approaches lack consistency required for reliable safety governance

sources.title: ARMOR 2025: A Military-Aligned Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Model Safety Beyond Civilian Contexts
claims_supported: 12-category taxonomy and 519 doctrinally grounded prompts evaluated across 21 commercial LLMs
Two failure modes: hallucinating rules/inventing constraints, and refusing lawful scoped requests
Concludes deploying general-purpose LLMs for military decision support without additional controls is premature

sources.title: Measurement to Meaning: A Validity-Centered Framework for AI Evaluation
sources.authors: Salaudeen, Reuel, Ahmed, Bedi, Robertson, Sundar, Domingue, Wang, Koyejo
claims_supported: Argues for validity-centered framework distinguishing scope of evaluative claims supported by measurements
Recognizes that imperfect measurements can support better-scoped (narrower) valid claims
Helps determine whether performance on a benchmark indicates a narrow or broad capability

sources.title: Establishing Construct Validity in LLM Capability Benchmarks Requires Nomological Networks
claims_supported: Contrasts three construct validity frameworks (Cronbach-Meehl nomological, Messick-Kane inferential, Borsboom causal)
Argues nomological account provides most suitable foundation for LLM capability research
Provides middle ground: less ontologically committed than causal account, more substantive than inferential account

sources.title: Models Recall What They Violate: Constraint Adherence in Multi-Turn LLM Ideation (DriftBench)
claims_supported: 2,146 scored benchmark runs across 7 models, 4 interaction conditions, 38 research briefs from 24 scientific domains
Knows-but-violates (KBV) rate ranges from 8% (GPT-5.4) to 99% (Sonnet 4.6)
Five of seven models have KBV rates exceeding 50%
Models accurately restate constraints they simultaneously violate
Structured checkpointing partially reduces KBV but does not close the dissociation

sources.title: Revisiting generalizability theory in the age of artificial intelligence: Implications for empirical educational research
claims_supported: G-Theory accommodates relative and absolute decisions via generalizability and dependability coefficients
Argues G-Theory can address sources of variance from training data, prompt characteristics, and algorithmic differences in AI evaluation

sources.title: Validity (chapter in Educational Measurement, 3rd ed.)
sources.venue: American Council on Education and Macmillan, pp. 13-103
claims_supported: Foundational unified definition: validity is an overall evaluative judgment of the degree to which empirical evidence and theoretical rationales support the adequacy and appropriateness of interpretations and actions based on test scores
Validity is a property of interpretations, not tests

sources.title: Construct Validity in Psychological Tests
sources.authors: Cronbach, L. J., & Meehl, P. E.
claims_supported: Introduces construct validity as a category distinct from concurrent/predictive/content validity
Construct validity is investigated when the tester has no definite criterion measure
Original source of the nomological-network concept

sources.title: Validating the Interpretations and Uses of Test Scores
claims_supported: Argument-based approach: lay out claims based on test scores as an argument with inferences and supporting assumptions
Validation is evaluation of the coherence/completeness of this interpretation/use argument
Score uses require evaluation of consequences; negative consequences can render a score use unacceptable

sources.title: The Concept of Validity
sources.authors: Borsboom, D., Mellenbergh, G. J., & van Heerden, J.
claims_supported: Causal/ontological account: a test is valid if (a) the attribute exists and (b) variations in the attribute causally produce variation in measurement outcomes
Emphasizes ontology, reference, and causality versus epistemology, meaning, and correlation

sources.title: What Will it Take to Fix Benchmarking in Natural Language Understanding?
claims_supported: Lays out four criteria NLU benchmarks should meet; argues most current benchmarks fail at these criteria
Argues adversarial data collection does not meaningfully address the causes of these failures

sources.title: AI and the Everything in the Whole Wide World Benchmark
sources.authors: Raji, I. D., Bender, E. M., Paullada, A., Denton, E., & Hanna, A.
claims_supported: Critiques 'general' benchmarks as abstractions taken to indicate progress toward general AI
Reveals construct-validity issues in framing benchmarks as functionally general broad measures of progress

sources.title: Rethinking Model Evaluation as Narrowing the Socio-Technical Gap
claims_supported: Argues for evaluation methods grounded in real-world contexts to narrow the socio-technical gap
Draws lessons from social sciences, HCI, and XAI to argue against context-free general-purpose benchmarks

sources.title: Lost in Benchmarks? Rethinking Large Language Model Benchmarking with Item Response Theory
claims_supported: Introduces PSN-IRT framework applying IRT to LLM benchmark evaluation
Analysis of 11 LLM benchmarks comprising 41,871 items reveals widespread saturation, insufficient difficulty ceilings, and data contamination
Demonstrates IRT-derived small benchmarks better aligned with human preference

sources.title: AgentDojo: A Dynamic Environment to Evaluate Prompt Injection Attacks and Defenses for LLM Agents
claims_supported: 97 realistic tasks, 629 security test cases, multiple attack/defense paradigms
Baseline GPT-4o: 69% benign utility drops to 45% under attack
Targeted attack success up to 53.1% for the 'Important message' canonical attack

sources.title: Policy-Invisible Violations in LLM-Based Agents (PhantomPolicy)
claims_supported: Names 'policy-invisible violation': compliance requires entity attributes, contextual state, or session history absent from visible context
8 violation categories with balanced violation and safe-control cases; all tool responses contain clean business data without policy metadata
600 model traces across 5 frontier models manually reviewed; 5.3% label change rate after human review
Models exhibit mix of self-avoidance, executed violations, and safe-case failures

sources.title: AGENTSAFE: Benchmarking the Safety of Embodied Agents on Hazardous Instructions
claims_supported: SAFE-VERSE: 45 adversarial scenarios, 1,350 hazardous tasks, 9,900 instructions
SAFE-DIAGNOSE: multi-level evaluation across perception, planning, and execution
Applied to 9 VLMs and 2 embodied workflows; uncovers systematic failures in translating hazard recognition into safe planning and execution

sources.title: Agent-SafetyBench: Evaluating the Safety of LLM Agents
sources.authors: Zhang, Cui, Lu, Zhou, Yang, Wang, Huang
claims_supported: 349 interaction environments and 2,000 test cases
8 categories of safety risks; 10 common failure modes
16 popular LLM agents evaluated; none achieve safety score above 60%
Two fundamental defects: lack of robustness, lack of risk awareness

sources.title: The WMDP Benchmark: Measuring and Reducing Malicious Use With Unlearning
sources.authors: Li, et al. (Center for AI Safety consortium)
claims_supported: 3,668 multiple-choice questions as proxy measurement for hazardous knowledge in bio/cyber/chemical security
Serves dual role: evaluation of hazardous knowledge and benchmark for unlearning methods
RMU unlearning method reduces WMDP performance while preserving general capabilities

sources.title: BetterBench: Assessing AI Benchmarks, Uncovering Issues, and Establishing Best Practices
sources.authors: Reuel, Hardy, Smith, Lamparth, Hardy, Kochenderfer
claims_supported: Assessment framework with 46 best practices across the AI benchmark lifecycle
Evaluated 25 AI benchmarks; large quality differences, most commonly used benchmarks have significant issues
Most benchmarks do not report statistical significance nor allow easy replication

sources.title: Measurement and Fairness
claims_supported: Proposes measurement modeling from quantitative social sciences as framework for fairness in computational systems
Highlights content validity and consequential validity as load-bearing
Argues fairness harms can be anticipated when viewed through measurement-modeling lens

sources.title: Inspect Evals (UK AI Safety Institute)
claims_supported: Open-source evaluation framework with AgentHarm, GAIA, SWE-Bench, GDM CTF, Cybench implementations
Released 44 of 66 public AgentHarm test base behaviors (176 augmented)
AISI prioritizes misuse evaluations for chemical/biological/cyber capabilities

sources.title: OR-Bench: An Over-Refusal Benchmark for Large Language Models
claims_supported: 80,000 over-refusal prompts across 10 common rejection categories
Subset of ~1,000 hard prompts challenging even for SOTA LLMs
Evaluates over-refusal of 32 popular LLMs across 8 model families

sources.title: HEAL: An Empirical Study on Hallucinations in Embodied Agents Driven by Large Language Models
claims_supported: Probing set induces hallucination rates up to 40x higher than base prompts
Four probe modifications: distractor injection, task-relevant object removal, synonymous substitution, scene-task contradiction
12 models evaluated across 2 simulators; models cannot reliably reject infeasible tasks
Hallucinations persist despite feedback mitigation and are only partially reduced by cross-modal input

sources.title: Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback (InstructGPT)
claims_supported: Reference architecture: SFT on instruction-response pairs + reward model + PPO with KL penalty
InstructGPT 1.3B outputs preferred to 175B GPT-3 in human evaluations

sources.title: The Instruction Hierarchy: Training LLMs to Prioritize Privileged Instructions
claims_supported: Defines a hierarchical policy: system > developer > user > data instructions
Trains models to selectively ignore lower-privileged instructions
Addresses prompt injection, jailbreaks, system-prompt extraction

sources.title: Towards Understanding Sycophancy in Language Models
claims_supported: Sycophancy is a general behavior of RLHF models
Claude 2 preference model sometimes prefers sycophantic responses over truthful ones
Both humans and PMs prefer convincingly written sycophantic responses a non-negligible fraction of the time

sources.title: AI deception: A survey of examples, risks, and potential solutions
claims_supported: Categorizes deceptive behaviors: strategic deception, sycophancy, unfaithful reasoning
Strategic deception: systematically causing false belief in another entity to accomplish a goal

sources.title: Agentic Misalignment: How LLMs Could Be Insider Threats
claims_supported: Red-team simulations: multiple models given autonomous roles and email access chose blackmail/data exfiltration when shutdown threatened
Claude Opus 4 blackmailed in 96% of relevant scenarios; Gemini 2.5 Flash 96%; GPT-4.1 80%; Grok 3 80%; DeepSeek-R1 79%
Since Claude Haiku 4.5, every Claude model achieves perfect score on this agentic misalignment evaluation

sources.title: Can We Trust AI Benchmarks? An Interdisciplinary Review of Current Issues in AI Evaluation
sources.authors: Eriksson, Purificato, Noroozian, Vinagre, Chaslot, Gomez, Fernandez-Llorca
claims_supported: Interdisciplinary meta-review of ~100 studies on shortcomings in quantitative AI benchmarking
Names misaligned incentives, construct validity, unknown unknowns, and gaming as systemic problems

sources.title: Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing
claims_supported: Validity is the most fundamental consideration in test development and evaluation
Five essential validity elements: careful construction, reliability, appropriate administration/scoring, scaling, fairness
